lessons.title: Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete
lessons.summary_md:
So far, every variable you have created lives on the **stack** -- its lifetime is tied to the scope where it is defined, and the compiler handles cleanup automatically. **Dynamic memory allocation** lets you request memory from the **heap** (also called the free store) at runtime, giving you explicit control over when that memory is created and destroyed.

Use the `new` keyword to allocate a single object on the heap. It returns a pointer to the newly created memory. Use `delete` to release that memory when you are done with it.

```cpp
#include <iostream>

int main()
{
    int* ptr{ new int{ 42 } }; // allocate an int on the heap
    std::cout << *ptr << '\n'; // prints 42
    delete ptr;                // free the heap memory
    ptr = nullptr;             // prevent dangling pointer
    return 0;
}
```

Three critical mistakes to avoid:

- **Memory leak:** If you lose the pointer to heap memory (e.g., the pointer goes out of scope or gets reassigned) without calling `delete`, that memory is never freed. The OS reclaims it when the program exits, but long-running programs will consume more and more memory.
- **Dangling pointer:** After `delete`, the pointer still holds the old address. Dereferencing it is undefined behavior. Always set the pointer to `nullptr` immediately after `delete`.
- **Double delete:** Calling `delete` on the same non-null pointer twice is undefined behavior. Setting the pointer to `nullptr` after delete also guards against this, because `delete nullptr` is a safe no-op.

When should you use dynamic allocation? When the size or number of objects is not known at compile time, or when an object must outlive the scope that created it. Stack memory is faster and safer by default -- prefer it unless you have a concrete reason to allocate on the heap.

`new` can fail if the system runs out of memory. By default it throws a `std::bad_alloc` exception. For now, assume allocations succeed in small programs.

exercises.title: Heap-Allocated Sum
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Read two integers from input, dynamically allocate an `int` on the heap to store their sum, print the result, and properly clean up the allocated memory.

## Input

- A single line containing two space-separated integers `a` and `b` (-10000 <= a, b <= 10000).

## Output

- Line 1: the value pointed to by the heap-allocated pointer (the sum of `a` and `b`).
- Line 2: the literal text `nullptr` if the pointer is null after cleanup, or `dangling` otherwise.

## Requirements

- Use `new` to allocate a single `int` on the heap and initialize it with the sum.
- Use `delete` to free the memory.
- Set the pointer to `nullptr` after deleting.
- Print whether the pointer is `nullptr` after cleanup to confirm correct practice.

exercises.title: Dynamic Swap
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Read two integers, store each in a separately heap-allocated `int`, swap the values they point to, print the swapped values, and clean up all allocated memory.

## Input

- A single line containing two space-separated integers `x` and `y` (-10000 <= x, y <= 10000).

## Output

- Line 1: the value pointed to by the first pointer after swapping (originally `y`).
- Line 2: the value pointed to by the second pointer after swapping (originally `x`).
- Line 3: `clean` if both pointers are `nullptr` after cleanup.

## Requirements

- Use `new` to allocate two separate `int` objects on the heap, one initialized with `x` and one with `y`.
- Swap the pointed-to values using a temporary variable (do not swap the pointers themselves).
- After printing, `delete` both allocations and set both pointers to `nullptr`.
- Print `clean` only if both pointers are `nullptr` after cleanup.

lessons.title: Dynamically allocating arrays

exercises.title: Dynamic Array Reversal
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Dynamically allocate an array of integers, read values into it, reverse the array in place, and print the result.

## Input

- Line 1: a single integer `n` (1 <= n <= 100), the number of elements.
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers.

## Output

- A single line containing the reversed elements, each separated by a space, with a trailing space after the last element.

## Requirements

- Allocate the array using `new int[n]{}`.
- Reverse the array in place by swapping elements from the outside in (use `std::swap` from `<utility>`).
- Print the reversed array.
- Free the memory using `delete[]` before the program ends.
- Do not use `std::vector`, `std::array`, or any standard library algorithm for reversing.

exercises.title: Merge Two Dynamic Arrays
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Read two dynamically allocated arrays of sorted integers, merge them into a third dynamically allocated array in sorted order, and print the result.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `a` (1 <= a <= 50), the size of the first array.
- Line 2: `a` space-separated integers in non-decreasing order.
- Line 3: an integer `b` (1 <= b <= 50), the size of the second array.
- Line 4: `b` space-separated integers in non-decreasing order.

## Output

- A single line containing `a + b` integers in non-decreasing order, each separated by a space, with a trailing space after the last element.

## Requirements

- Allocate all three arrays using `new int[size]{}`.
- Use two index variables to walk through both input arrays simultaneously, always picking the smaller current element for the merged array.
- After one input array is exhausted, copy the remaining elements from the other.
- Print the merged array, then free all three arrays with `delete[]`.
- Do not use `std::vector`, `std::array`, or any standard library algorithm for merging or sorting.

test_cases.label: Basic merge of two small arrays

lessons.title: Destructors

exercises.title: RAII String Wrapper
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Implement a class `DynString` that owns a dynamically allocated `char` array and cleans it up in its destructor, demonstrating the RAII pattern.

## Input
- Line 1: a single integer `N` (1 <= N <= 100), the number of characters to store.
- Line 2: exactly `N` space-separated characters.

## Output
- Line 1: `Stored: ` followed by all N characters with no separators.
- Line 2: `Destroyed N chars` where N is the count, printed by the destructor.

## Requirements
1. `DynString` has a constructor taking an `int` size that allocates a `char` array with `new char[size]`.
2. `DynString` has a destructor that calls `delete[]` on the array and prints the destruction message.
3. Provide a `void set(int index, char c)` member to set individual characters.
4. Provide a `void print() const` member that prints all characters in order with no separators.
5. Create the `DynString` as a local variable in `main` so the destructor runs automatically at the end of scope.
6. Do not use `std::string` or smart pointers.

exercises.title: Reverse Destruction Order
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Demonstrate that local objects are destroyed in reverse order of construction by creating multiple RAII wrapper objects and observing destructor output.

## Input
- A single line containing exactly 3 space-separated integers representing sizes for three dynamic arrays.

## Output
- Lines 1-3: `Created pool 'X' (N ints)` for each pool in creation order, where X is A, B, C and N is the size.
- Lines 4-6: `Destroyed pool 'X' (N ints)` for each pool in reverse creation order (C, B, A).

## Requirements
1. Implement a class `IntPool` with a constructor that takes a `char` label and an `int` size, allocates an `int` array with `new int[size]{}`, and prints the creation message.
2. The destructor calls `delete[]` on the array and prints the destruction message.
3. In `main`, create three `IntPool` objects named `a`, `b`, `c` (labels `'A'`, `'B'`, `'C'`) as local variables in that order.
4. Do not explicitly call any destructor. Let scope exit handle cleanup.
5. Do not use smart pointers or `std::vector`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- sizes 3 5 2
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Created pool 'A' (3 ints)
Created pool 'B' (5 ints)
Created pool 'C' (2 ints)
Destroyed pool 'C' (2 ints)
Destroyed pool 'B' (5 ints)
Destroyed pool 'A' (3 ints)

test_cases.label: Hidden -- all same size
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Created pool 'A' (10 ints)
Created pool 'B' (10 ints)
Created pool 'C' (10 ints)
Destroyed pool 'C' (10 ints)
Destroyed pool 'B' (10 ints)
Destroyed pool 'A' (10 ints)

test_cases.label: Hidden -- size one each
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Created pool 'A' (1 ints)
Created pool 'B' (1 ints)
Created pool 'C' (1 ints)
Destroyed pool 'C' (1 ints)
Destroyed pool 'B' (1 ints)
Destroyed pool 'A' (1 ints)

lessons.title: Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

exercises.title: Build and Print a Dynamic 2D Array
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Dynamically allocate a 2D integer array, fill it with values read from input, and print it row by row. Properly deallocate all memory.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `rows` and `cols` (1 <= rows, cols <= 10)
- Next `rows` lines: `cols` space-separated integers per line

## Output

- Print the matrix row by row. Within each row, separate values with a single space. Print a newline after each row.

## Requirements

- Declare an `int**` and use `new` to allocate an array of `int*` pointers, then allocate each row with `new int[cols]`.
- After printing, deallocate each row with `delete[]`, then deallocate the array of pointers with `delete[]`.
- Do not use `std::vector` or any container class.

exercises.title: Transpose a Dynamic Matrix
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Read a matrix, build its **transpose** in a second dynamically allocated 2D array, and print the transposed matrix. The transpose of an `R x C` matrix is a `C x R` matrix where element `[r][c]` becomes element `[c][r]`.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `R` and `C` (1 <= R, C <= 10)
- Next `R` lines: `C` space-separated integers per line

## Output

- Print the transposed matrix (`C` rows, `R` columns). Within each row, separate values with a single space. Print a newline after each row.

## Requirements

- Allocate the original matrix as `int**` with `new`.
- Allocate a second `int**` for the transpose (dimensions `C x R`).
- Copy values so that `transposed[c][r] = original[r][c]`.
- After printing, deallocate both matrices completely (rows first, then the pointer arrays).
- Do not use `std::vector` or any container class.

lessons.title: Void pointers

exercises.title: Cast and Print via Void Pointer
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Read an integer and a double from input. Store pointers to each in `void*` variables, then cast them back to their original types to print the values.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer
- Line 2: a double (printed with fixed 2-decimal precision)

## Output

- Line 1: `int: ` followed by the integer value
- Line 2: `double: ` followed by the double value with exactly 2 decimal places

## Requirements

- Declare an `int` variable and a `double` variable, reading into each from `std::cin`.
- Assign their addresses to two separate `void*` variables.
- Use `static_cast` to convert each `void*` back to the correct typed pointer, then dereference to print.
- Use `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(2)` from `<iomanip>` for the double output.

exercises.title: Generic Swap Through Void Pointers
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement a function `swapBytes` that swaps the raw memory contents of two objects given their addresses as `void*` and their size in bytes. Use it to swap two integers.

## Input

- Line 1: two space-separated integers `a` and `b`

## Output

- Line 1: `Before: a b` (the original values, space-separated)
- Line 2: `After: b a` (the swapped values, space-separated)

## Requirements

- Write a function with the signature: `void swapBytes(void* p1, void* p2, std::size_t size)`.
- Inside `swapBytes`, cast both `void*` parameters to `char*` so you can iterate byte by byte.
- Swap each corresponding byte using a temporary `char` variable. Do not use `std::swap` or `std::memcpy`.
- In `main`, read two ints, print the before line, call `swapBytes` with `sizeof(int)`, then print the after line.
